# 2 H. Cao et al.

detection [1], autonomous driving [2], and collision avoidance. These applications rely on well-trained deep neural network (DNN) models to perform inference on real-time input data. The process of inference will consume a lot of computing resources. Although the computing capabilities of modern vehicles have been greatly improved, they still struggle to support a large number of the inference requests. We use the Jetson Xavier NX, a common on-board computing device, as an example. When using the ResNet-152 model to infer a one-second video, the Jetson Xavier NX takes approximately 4.5 seconds [3]. However, this processing time is far from sufficient to meet the real-time demands of inference tasks. As a solution, vehicular edge computing (VEC) has emerged as a promising paradigm, where inference tasks can be offloaded to nearby roadside units (RSUs) with abundant computing resources [4].

Effective model deployment and task offloading strategies can significantly improve quality of service (QoS) for inference tasks [5]. Model deployment refers to deploying well-trained DNN models on RSUs to serve the corresponding inference tasks. When the inference task arrives at the RSU, if the requested model is not available, the corresponding model needs to be deployed. This will introduce additional model deployment delay. Therefore, model deployment directly impacts the QoS of inference tasks [6]. Task offloading refers to scheduling an inference task from one RSU to another with more computing resources to accelerate the execution, thereby improving the QoS for inference tasks [7]. Jointly optimizing model deployment and task offloading can more effectively enhance QoS. In the optimization process, these two decisions are coupled. Specifically, the decision on one directly affects the outcome of the other. Model deployment is influenced by distribution of inference tasks, while task offloading depends on whether the required model is available at the target RSU. Therefore, coordinating both is crucial to improve QoS for inference tasks.

In VEC, load balancing can effectively optimize the utilization of system computing resources and improve system throughput [8]. The computational resources of RSUs are limited. When a large number of inference tasks arrive simultaneously, the computational resources of RSUs are under significant pressure, resulting in a bottleneck of processing capacity. In this case, the lack of an effective load balancing mechanism may make some RSUs overloaded while others remain underutilized. This imbalance not only increases inference task response delay but also degrades the overall system performance [9]. To address this issue, load balancing strategies for DNN inference in VEC are crucial. However, this is challenging due to several factors. First, RSUs often have different computing units (e.g., CPUs and GPUs) with varying processing powers. Second, inference tasks are unpredictable and delay-sensitive. These factors make load balancing for DNN inference in VEC more difficult than in traditional edge computing environments.

In conclusion, joint optimization of model deployment and task offloading can effectively improve the QoS of inference tasks. However, few existing works focus on load balancing for DNN inference in VEC, which results in low resource utilization and poor QoS. For load balancing, existing works focus on general
